ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a clearly new perspective—using spatial attention maps (both activation- and gradient-based) as the medium for teacher→student knowledge transfer—distinct from prior full-activation/hint-based methods. The techniques (several attention map definitions, l2-normalized matching loss, and a gradient-based double-backprop variant) are non-trivial extensions and are empirically shown to outperform FitNets-style transfer and combine with knowledge distillation across multiple datasets and architectures. The authors explicitly compare to and differentiate from prior work (FitNets, KD) and report thorough experiments, so the contribution is notably novel though not a radical departure from existing distillation literature. | B(4): The paper introduces a new perspective—using spatial attention maps (activation- and gradient-based) as the mechanism for teacher→student transfer—which is a clear departure from prior full-activation (FitNets-style) and KD approaches. It defines concrete attention mappings and corresponding transfer losses, and empirically demonstrates advantages across datasets and architectures (including outperforming FitNets and combining with KD). These elements represent a non-trivial methodological extension and are clearly differentiated from prior hint/KD work, so the contribution is notably novel though not a radical paradigm shift.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new perspective—using spatial attention maps (both activation- and gradient-based) as the medium for teacher→student knowledge transfer—distinct from prior full-activation/hint-based methods. The techniques (several attention map definitions, l2-normalized matching loss, and a gradient-based double-backprop variant) are non-trivial extensions and are empirically shown to outperform FitNets-style transfer and combine with knowledge distillation across multiple datasets and architectures. The authors explicitly compare to and differentiate from prior work (FitNets, KD) and report thorough experiments, so the contribution is notably novel though not a radical departure from existing distillation literature.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a new perspective—using spatial attention maps (activation- and gradient-based) as the mechanism for teacher→student transfer—which is a clear departure from prior full-activation (FitNets-style) and KD approaches. It defines concrete attention mappings and corresponding transfer losses, and empirically demonstrates advantages across datasets and architectures (including outperforming FitNets and combining with KD). These elements represent a non-trivial methodological extension and are clearly differentiated from prior hint/KD work, so the contribution is notably novel though not a radical paradigm shift.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (method definitions followed by extensive experiments on CIFAR, ImageNet, CUB, Scenes and visualizations) and clearly states its contributions early (activation- and gradient-based attention transfer, and how losses are applied). Notation and method descriptions are presented clearly (different attention-map formulations and loss terms are defined), and figures/tables/visualizations appear informative across multiple datasets. Minor presentation issues remain: some limitations are noted (e.g., small-input spatial resolution, unclear interaction with batch-norm for gradient AT, and limited ImageNet hyperparameter tuning) which slightly temper clarity but do not impede understanding. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents its main contributions early (activation- and gradient-based attention transfer) with a clear methods section and a wide-ranging experimental evaluation across CIFAR, ImageNet, CUB, Scenes and visualizations. Notation and definitions for the attention maps and loss terms are presented coherently, and figures/tables/visualizations appear effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, but there are minor presentation gaps insofar as some practical interactions (e.g., batch-normalization during the second backprop) and limited ImageNet hyperparameter tuning are only briefly discussed, which slightly reduces clarity for practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (method definitions followed by extensive experiments on CIFAR, ImageNet, CUB, Scenes and visualizations) and clearly states its contributions early (activation- and gradient-based attention transfer, and how losses are applied). Notation and method descriptions are presented clearly (different attention-map formulations and loss terms are defined), and figures/tables/visualizations appear informative across multiple datasets. Minor presentation issues remain: some limitations are noted (e.g., small-input spatial resolution, unclear interaction with batch-norm for gradient AT, and limited ImageNet hyperparameter tuning) which slightly temper clarity but do not impede understanding.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and presents its main contributions early (activation- and gradient-based attention transfer) with a clear methods section and a wide-ranging experimental evaluation across CIFAR, ImageNet, CUB, Scenes and visualizations. Notation and definitions for the attention maps and loss terms are presented coherently, and figures/tables/visualizations appear effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, but there are minor presentation gaps insofar as some practical interactions (e.g., batch-normalization during the second backprop) and limited ImageNet hyperparameter tuning are only briefly discussed, which slightly reduces clarity for practitioners.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by extensive experiments across multiple datasets and architectures (CIFAR-10, ImageNet, CUB, MIT Scenes) and comparisons to sensible baselines (plain student, KD, FitNets/full-activation transfer). The attention definitions and training losses (l2-normalized attention-map matching and gradient-based double-backprop) are clearly specified, code and hyperparameters are provided, and activation-based AT is reported to outperform FitNets. Minor technical concerns remain: gradient-based attention transfer requires a second backpropagation with unclear interaction with batch normalization, and KD failing or hurting convergence on ImageNet for some teacher/student pairs indicates sensitivity to architecture mismatch; limited spatial resolution on small inputs is also acknowledged. These issues are implementation/robustness concerns that do not invalidate the main experimental conclusions. | B(4): The main claims (attention-based transfer methods, activation- and gradient-based attention maps, and consistent empirical improvements across CIFAR, ImageNet, CUB and other datasets) are clearly stated and supported by experiments versus sensible baselines (KD, FitNets-style hints) with code and hyperparameters provided. The mathematical definitions (channel-wise sums/p-sums/p-max and gradient-based double backprop) are straightforward and appear correct; no formal proofs were required. Experimental methodology is generally sound (multiple datasets, architectures, ablations), though the paper notes limited ImageNet hyperparameter tuning and does not report statistical significance tests; the gradient-based method also has an unresolved interaction with batch-norm during the second backprop. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor methodological caveats that do not undermine the main claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by extensive experiments across multiple datasets and architectures (CIFAR-10, ImageNet, CUB, MIT Scenes) and comparisons to sensible baselines (plain student, KD, FitNets/full-activation transfer). The attention definitions and training losses (l2-normalized attention-map matching and gradient-based double-backprop) are clearly specified, code and hyperparameters are provided, and activation-based AT is reported to outperform FitNets. Minor technical concerns remain: gradient-based attention transfer requires a second backpropagation with unclear interaction with batch normalization, and KD failing or hurting convergence on ImageNet for some teacher/student pairs indicates sensitivity to architecture mismatch; limited spatial resolution on small inputs is also acknowledged. These issues are implementation/robustness concerns that do not invalidate the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (attention-based transfer methods, activation- and gradient-based attention maps, and consistent empirical improvements across CIFAR, ImageNet, CUB and other datasets) are clearly stated and supported by experiments versus sensible baselines (KD, FitNets-style hints) with code and hyperparameters provided. The mathematical definitions (channel-wise sums/p-sums/p-max and gradient-based double backprop) are straightforward and appear correct; no formal proofs were required. Experimental methodology is generally sound (multiple datasets, architectures, ablations), though the paper notes limited ImageNet hyperparameter tuning and does not report statistical significance tests; the gradient-based method also has an unresolved interaction with batch-norm during the second backprop. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor methodological caveats that do not undermine the main claims.

main_claims: Propose attention as a mechanism for transferring knowledge from one network to another.
Introduce both activation-based and gradient-based spatial attention maps for defining attention in CNNs and methods to transfer them from teacher to student.
Show experimentally that attention transfer yields significant improvements across multiple datasets and architectures (residual and non-residual).
Show that activation-based attention transfer outperforms full-activation (FitNets-style) transfer and can be combined with knowledge distillation.
_summary.methodology: Define spatial attention maps from CNN hidden activations (activation-based: sum, p-sum, p-max over channels) and from gradients w.r.t. input (gradient-based), then train a student by adding losses that match l2-normalized teacher and student attention maps (activation-based) or by matching input gradients via a double backpropagation term (gradient-based).
limitations: Limited spatial resolution for very small inputs (e.g., CIFAR 32×32) reduces attention map resolution, though AT still gives benefits.
Knowledge Distillation (KD) did not work well on ImageNet for the ResNet-18/ResNet-34 pair and can hurt convergence when teacher and student have different depth/architecture.
Gradient-based attention transfer requires a second (mixed) backpropagation; interaction with batch normalization during the second backpropagation is unclear.
ImageNet experiments were run without extensive hyperparameter tuning (authors state they did not tune hyperparameters for ImageNet).
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - deep learning (attention transfer / knowledge distillation for CNNs)
